prompt: Write a 150-word short story about a lighthouse keeper who discovers something unexpected in the fog. Do not use dialogue.
response: Each dusk, Keeper Rowan wound the brass clockwork and trimmed the wick, sending a blade of light across the Atlantic as if stitching sea to sky. One pearl-gray dawn, the horn lowed but the beam snagged on something solid inside the murk: a ship that should not exist, its masts feathered with salt crystals, decks bright with unrusted brass. Rowan descended the spiral stairs, boots ringing like slow bells. On the wet shale he found a single wet footprint larger than any man’s, toes webbed, heel glowing faintly. The tide exhaled; the fog swallowed the vessel whole. When he climbed back, the lamp room smelled of drowned roses. In the logbook he wrote only: *Visitor came aboard the light.* Then he released the brake, letting the great lens spin unchecked, scattering warnings he no longer trusted. Outside, the horn kept answering itself, as though the sea had begun to speak in his absent voice.
